ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a notable and clearly-differentiated idea—using a multivariate Gaussian as an attention/scoring function (Eq.1) and modeling relations as Gaussians (means + covariances) so that path composition corresponds to mean translation and variance addition and conjunction to Gaussian product. While Gaussian embeddings exist (Vilnis & McCallum 2014) and compositional training of TransE was explored (Guu et al. 2015), applying Gaussian attention to content-based neural memory access and QA (propagating uncertainty and naturally handling conjunctions) is a non-trivial, original extension that an expert would find informative though not a radical departure from prior methods.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow (Intro → Method in Sec.2 → QA model Sec.3 → Experiments Sec.4 → Conclusion), and the main contribution (the Gaussian attention model and its use for KB embedding and QA) is stated early in the abstract and introduction. Notation is introduced and used consistently (definitions of E, R, v_s, δ_r, Σ_r and scoring eqs. (1)–(7) are clear) and the running example (“Who plays forward for Borussia Dortmund?”) helps illustrate the method. Presentation issues are minor but notable: many figures/tables are referenced (Fig.1–4, Tables 1–11) but not shown here, and the document contains duplicated/repeated text and some formatting glitches in the Appendix, which slightly detract from readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The core mathematical formulation is correct and internally consistent: attention as log Gaussian likelihood (Eq.1), relation embedding as translation+covariance (Eq.2), compositional mean/covariance µ = v_s+Σδ_r and Σ = Σ_r summed along a path (Eq.4) and conjunction as product of Gaussians (Eq.7) follow standard Gaussian algebra. Training objectives and parametrization (diagonal Σ via ELU, ranking loss Eq.3 and QA loss) are reasonable and implemented consistently. Minor concerns: the use of continuous attention coefficients αr and the choice Σ_e = Σ α_r^2 Σ_r (Eq.6) is heuristic and assumes a scaling interpretation of α that is not fully justified; the model also relies on an oracle entity recognizer and evaluation is limited to a single, fairly small synthetic WorldCup2014 dataset with no statistical testing. Overall the claims are supported by correct derivations and experiments but scope and some assumptions should be better justified.
